content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:ef66a6e5

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: Past transcript contains durable personal history and stable preferences/criteria for pool selection, along with reusable context about why these criteria matter.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I / me / my; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements in the transcript refer to the stable user entity; no existing candidates are available.
- mention=local pool; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=CONCEPT; name=Local lap swimming pool; reason=The transcript repeatedly discusses finding a nearby pool for lap swimming as a reusable domain object, but no specific facility is named.
- mention=lap swimming; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=CONCEPT; name=Lap swimming; reason=Lap swimming is a central reusable concept in the transcript and has no existing candidate.
- mention=competitive swimming in college; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=CONCEPT; name=Competitive college swimming; reason=The user states prior experience swimming competitively in college; this is a reusable concept for later claims.
- mention=pool finder tools; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=SERVICE; name=Pool finder tools; reason=The transcript discusses pool finder tools generically as services for locating pools.
- mention=Swim.com; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=SERVICE; name=Swim.com; reason=Swim.com is a named service mentioned as a pool finder tool.
- mention=PoolFinder; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=SERVICE; name=PoolFinder; reason=PoolFinder is a named service mentioned as a pool finder tool.
- mention=USA Swimming; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=USA Swimming; reason=USA Swimming is a named organization discussed in relation to pool finding and swim programs.
- mention=USA Swimming's Pool Finder; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=SERVICE; name=USA Swimming Pool Finder; reason=The transcript refers to a specific pool finder tool offered by USA Swimming.
- mention=YMCA; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<9>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=YMCA; reason=YMCA is a named organization type/source for pools and swim programs.
- mention=masters swimming programs; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<10>; type=CONCEPT; name=Masters swimming programs; reason=The transcript repeatedly discusses joining or filtering for masters swimming programs.
- mention=USMS / United States Masters Swimming; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<11>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=United States Masters Swimming; reason=USMS is expanded in the transcript as United States Masters Swimming and is a named organization.
- mention=swim teams; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<12>; type=CONCEPT; name=Swim teams; reason=Swim teams are discussed as programs available at pools, not as one specific team.
- mention=kickboards, pull buoys, and other equipment; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<13>; type=CONCEPT; name=Swimming training equipment; reason=The transcript discusses access to workout equipment as a reusable concept.
- mention=lane sharing and circle swimming; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<14>; type=CONCEPT; name=Lane sharing and circle swimming; reason=The transcript discusses these as pool policies relevant to lap swimming.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<15>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|local pool|lap swimming|masters swimming|pool finder tools
        text_queries=pool selection preferences lap swimming morning 25-yard 78-80°F masters swimming equipment lane sharing circle swimming|used to swim competitively in college looking to get back into swimming stay active relieve stress
        predicate_hints=has_experience_with|has_goal|prefers|has_constraint

LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/05/23 (Tue) 09:22
Transcript:
user: I'm looking to find a local pool that offers lap swimming hours. I used to swim competitively in college, and I'm looking to get back into it as a way to stay active and relieve stress.
assistant: That's great to hear! Lap swimming is an excellent way to stay active, relieve stress, and challenge yourself physically. I'd be happy to help you find a local pool that offers lap swimming hours.

Here are a few ways to find a pool near you:

1. **Google Search**: Type in "lap swimming near me" or "indoor lap pool near me" (if you prefer an indoor pool) along with your city or zip code. This should give you a list of nearby pools that offer lap swimming.
2. **Pool Finder Tools**: Websites like **Swim.com** or **PoolFinder** allow you to search for pools by location, pool type, and amenities (like lap lanes). You can also filter by hours of operation and fees.
3. **YMCA or Community Centers**: Many YMCAs and community centers have indoor pools that offer lap swimming hours. Check their websites or give them a call to inquire about their lap swimming schedule and fees.
4. **Local Colleges or Universities**: Reach out to the athletic department or recreation center at a nearby college or university to ask about lap swimming opportunities. They may offer community access to their pool or know of other local options.
5. **USA Swimming**: The official website of USA Swimming has a "Pool Finder" tool that allows you to search for pools in your area. You can filter by pool type, distance, and amenities.

Before visiting a pool, it's a good idea to:

* Check their website or call ahead to confirm lap swimming hours and availability.
* Ask about any membership or day pass fees.
* Inquire about lane availability and pool conditions (e.g., temperature, cleanliness).
* Find out if they have any special rules or regulations for lap swimmers.

Remember to also consider factors like parking, shower facilities, and locker room amenities when choosing a pool.

Good luck, and I hope you find a pool that meets your needs!
user: I'll try the pool finder tools you mentioned. Do you think I can filter the search by pools with morning lap swimming hours? I'm trying to fit swimming into my busy schedule, and morning sessions would work best for me.
assistant: Most pool finder tools and websites allow you to filter search results by various criteria, including hours of operation. You should be able to find pools that offer morning lap swimming hours.

Here are some tips to help you filter your search:

1. **Swim.com**: On Swim.com, you can filter search results by "Hours" and select "Morning" (or a specific time range, like 6:00 AM - 8:00 AM) to find pools that offer morning lap swimming hours.
2. **PoolFinder**: PoolFinder allows you to filter by "Hours of Operation" and select "Morning" or a specific time range. You can also filter by "Lap Swimming Hours" and select "Yes" to ensure the pool offers dedicated lap swimming time.
3. **USA Swimming's Pool Finder**: On USA Swimming's Pool Finder, you can filter search results by "Pool Hours" and select "Morning" or a specific time range. You can also check the "Lap Swimming" box to find pools that offer lap swimming opportunities.

When searching, you can also try using keywords like "morning lap swim," "early bird swim," or "dawn patrol" to find pools that specifically offer morning lap swimming hours.

Additionally, consider the following:

* Be flexible with your schedule: If you can't find a pool with morning lap swimming hours, consider swimming during your lunch break or immediately after work.
* Check with local masters swimming programs: Many masters swimming programs offer morning workouts, and they might have access to pools that offer morning lap swimming hours.
* Contact pools directly: If you don't find a pool that meets your morning lap swimming needs, reach out to local pools directly and ask about their lap swimming hours and policies. They might be willing to accommodate your schedule or offer alternative solutions.

I hope this helps you find a pool that fits your busy schedule!
user: I've got a few more questions about the pool finder tools. Can I also filter by pool length and temperature? I used to swim competitively in college, and I'm looking for a 25-yard pool that's kept at around 78-80°F.
assistant: Yes, you can filter by pool length and temperature on many pool finder tools. Here's how:

1. **Swim.com**: On Swim.com, you can filter by "Pool Details" and select "Pool Length" to choose from various lengths, including 25 yards. You can also filter by "Water Temperature" and select a range (e.g., 78-80°F).
2. **PoolFinder**: PoolFinder allows you to filter by "Pool Characteristics" and select "Pool Length" to choose from various lengths, including 25 yards. You can also filter by "Pool Temperature" and select a range (e.g., 78-80°F).
3. **USA Swimming's Pool Finder**: On USA Swimming's Pool Finder, you can filter by "Pool Characteristics" and select "Pool Length" to choose from various lengths, including 25 yards. However, they don't have a specific filter for water temperature. You may need to contact the pool directly to inquire about their water temperature.

Additionally, you can also filter by other amenities that might be important to you, such as:

* **Lanes available**: Ensure the pool has enough lanes available for lap swimming during your preferred time.
* **Lane width**: If you're used to swimming in a specific lane width, you can filter by lane width to find pools that meet your preference.
* **Gutter system**: Some swimmers prefer pools with a specific gutter system, such as a bulkhead or a deck-level gutter.
* **Starting blocks**: If you're planning to do flip turns, you might want to filter by pools with starting blocks.
* **Shower and locker facilities**: Consider filtering by pools with modern shower and locker facilities, especially if you plan to swim regularly.

When searching, you can also try using keywords like "25-yard pool," "short course," "competition pool," or "lap pool" to find pools that are more likely to meet your needs.

Remember to always verify the pool's details with a phone call or visit to the pool's website to ensure they meet your requirements.
user: I'm also wondering if I can find pools that have swim teams or masters swimming programs that I can join. Do you know if the pool finder tools allow me to filter by that?
assistant: Many pool finder tools allow you to filter by pools with swim teams or masters swimming programs. Here's how:

1. **Swim.com**: On Swim.com, you can filter by "Programs & Teams" and select "Masters Swimming" or "Swim Team" to find pools with these programs. You can also filter by "USMS" (United States Masters Swimming) to find pools that are registered with USMS.
2. **PoolFinder**: PoolFinder allows you to filter by "Programs & Activities" and select "Masters Swimming" or "Swim Team" to find pools with these programs.
3. **USA Swimming's Pool Finder**: On USA Swimming's Pool Finder, you can filter by "Programs & Teams" and select "Masters Swimming" or "Swim Team" to find pools with these programs. You can also filter by "USMS" to find pools that are registered with USMS.

Additionally, you can also search for pools that are affiliated with specific swim organizations, such as:

* **USA Swimming**: The national governing body for swimming in the United States.
* **USMS**: United States Masters Swimming, a organization for adult swimmers.
* **YMCA**: Many YMCAs have swim teams and masters swimming programs.

When searching, you can also try using keywords like "masters swimming," "swim team," "USMS," or "USA Swimming" to find pools that are more likely to have these programs.

Some other tips:

* **Contact the pool directly**: Even if a pool doesn't show up in your search results, it's worth contacting them directly to ask about their swim teams or masters swimming programs. They might have programs that aren't listed online.
* **Check with local swim organizations**: Reach out to local swim organizations, such as your state or regional USMS chapter, to ask about pools with masters swimming programs in your area.
* **Look for online swim teams and clubs**: Join online forums, social media groups, or websites focused on swimming to connect with other swimmers and find pools with swim teams or masters swimming programs.

By filtering by pool amenities, programs, and affiliations, you should be able to find a pool that meets your needs and provides opportunities to join a swim team or masters swimming program.
user: I'd also like to ask about the pool's policy on kickboards, pull buoys, and other equipment. As someone who used to swim competitively in college, I'm used to having access to that kind of equipment during my workouts.
assistant: Many pool finder tools don't specifically ask about pool equipment, but you can still find out about a pool's policy on kickboards, pull buoys, and other equipment by:

1. **Contacting the pool directly**: Reach out to the pool via phone or email and ask about their policy on equipment usage. They can let you know what equipment is available, if it's included with membership or day passes, and if there are any specific rules or restrictions.
2. **Checking their website**: Sometimes, pools will list their equipment and amenities on their website. Look for a section on "Pool Amenities" or "Swim Equipment" to see if they mention kickboards, pull buoys, or other equipment.
3. **Asking about their masters swimming program**: If you're looking to join a masters swimming program, ask the coach or program administrator about the equipment they use during workouts. They can give you insight into what's available and what's included in the program.

Some questions to ask when inquiring about equipment:

* Do you have kickboards, pull buoys, and other swim equipment available for use during lap swimming hours?
* Is the equipment included with membership or day passes, or is there an additional fee?
* Are there any specific rules or restrictions on equipment usage, such as only using certain equipment during certain hours or with permission from the lifeguard?
* Do you have any equipment available for checkout or rental, or do I need to bring my own?
* Are there any plans to add new equipment or upgrade existing equipment in the near future?

By asking these questions, you can get a better sense of whether a pool's equipment policy will meet your needs and expectations.

Remember, it's always a good idea to confirm a pool's policies and amenities before visiting or joining, especially if you have specific requirements or preferences.
user: I'll also ask about their policy on lane sharing and circle swimming. As someone who's used to swimming competitively in college, I'm comfortable swimming in a fast-paced environment, but I want to make sure I'll have enough space to do my workouts effectively.
assistant: As a competitive swimmer, you're likely accustomed to swimming in a fast-paced environment, and lane sharing and circle swimming can be important considerations. Here's what you can ask about:

1. **Lane sharing**: Ask if the pool has a policy on lane sharing, especially during peak hours. Find out if they have designated lanes for fast, medium, and slow swimmers, and if they enforce these designations.
2. **Circle swimming**: Inquire about their circle swimming policy. Do they use a counterclockwise or clockwise rotation? Are there any specific rules or signals for passing or being passed by other swimmers?
3. **Lane availability**: Ask about the number of lanes available for lap swimming and if they're reserved for specific swimming levels (e.g., fast, medium, slow). Find out if they have any restrictions on lane usage during certain times of the day or week.
4. **Swimmer density**: Find out if the pool has a policy on swimmer density, meaning the number of swimmers allowed in each lane. This can impact your swimming experience, especially if you're used to swimming in a crowded college pool.
5. **Lane etiquette**: Ask about their expectations for lane etiquette, such as passing rules, swimming on the correct side of the lane, and respecting other swimmers' space.

Some sample questions to ask:

* What is your policy on lane sharing during peak hours?
* Do you have designated lanes for fast, medium, and slow swimmers?
* How do you handle circle swimming in the lanes? Is it counterclockwise or clockwise?
* Are there any specific rules or signals for passing or being passed by other swimmers?
* How many lanes are available for lap swimming, and are they reserved for specific swimming levels?
* Are there any restrictions on lane usage during certain times of the day or week?
* What is the typical swimmer density in each lane, and how do you enforce it?
* What are your expectations for lane etiquette, and how do you ensure swimmers follow these rules?

By asking these questions, you can get a sense of whether the pool's policies and environment will meet your needs and allow you to swim effectively.

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.
<env>
Project path: gromozeka-e2e-<id>
Is directory a git repo: No
Platform: Mac OS X
OS Version: 26.5.1
Today's date: <date>
</env>

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:ef66a6e5

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: Past transcript contains durable personal history and stable preferences/criteria for pool selection, along with reusable context about why these criteria matter.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Session date: 2023/05/23 (Tue) 09:22
Transcript:
user: I'm looking to find a local pool that offers lap swimming hours. I used to swim competitively in college, and I'm looking to get back into it as a way to stay active and relieve stress.
assistant: That's great to hear! Lap swimming is an excellent way to stay active, relieve stress, and challenge yourself physically. I'd be happy to help you find a local pool that offers lap swimming hours.

Here are a few ways to find a pool near you:

1. **Google Search**: Type in "lap swimming near me" or "indoor lap pool near me" (if you prefer an indoor pool) along with your city or zip code. This should give you a list of nearby pools that offer lap swimming.
2. **Pool Finder Tools**: Websites like **Swim.com** or **PoolFinder** allow you to search for pools by location, pool type, and amenities (like lap lanes). You can also filter by hours of operation and fees.
3. **YMCA or Community Centers**: Many YMCAs and community centers have indoor pools that offer lap swimming hours. Check their websites or give them a call to inquire about their lap swimming schedule and fees.
4. **Local Colleges or Universities**: Reach out to the athletic department or recreation center at a nearby college or university to ask about lap swimming opportunities. They may offer community access to their pool or know of other local options.
5. **USA Swimming**: The official website of USA Swimming has a "Pool Finder" tool that allows you to search for pools in your area. You can filter by pool type, distance, and amenities.

Before visiting a pool, it's a good idea to:

* Check their website or call ahead to confirm lap swimming hours and availability.
* Ask about any membership or day pass fees.
* Inquire about lane availability and pool conditions (e.g., temperature, cleanliness).
* Find out if they have any special rules or regulations for lap swimmers.

Remember to also consider factors like parking, shower facilities, and locker room amenities when choosing a pool.

Good luck, and I hope you find a pool that meets your needs!
user: I'll try the pool finder tools you mentioned. Do you think I can filter the search by pools with morning lap swimming hours? I'm trying to fit swimming into my busy schedule, and morning sessions would work best for me.
assistant: Most pool finder tools and websites allow you to filter search results by various criteria, including hours of operation. You should be able to find pools that offer morning lap swimming hours.

Here are some tips to help you filter your search:

1. **Swim.com**: On Swim.com, you can filter search results by "Hours" and select "Morning" (or a specific time range, like 6:00 AM - 8:00 AM) to find pools that offer morning lap swimming hours.
2. **PoolFinder**: PoolFinder allows you to filter by "Hours of Operation" and select "Morning" or a specific time range. You can also filter by "Lap Swimming Hours" and select "Yes" to ensure the pool offers dedicated lap swimming time.
3. **USA Swimming's Pool Finder**: On USA Swimming's Pool Finder, you can filter search results by "Pool Hours" and select "Morning" or a specific time range. You can also check the "Lap Swimming" box to find pools that offer lap swimming opportunities.

When searching, you can also try using keywords like "morning lap swim," "early bird swim," or "dawn patrol" to find pools that specifically offer morning lap swimming hours.

Additionally, consider the following:

* Be flexible with your schedule: If you can't find a pool with morning lap swimming hours, consider swimming during your lunch break or immediately after work.
* Check with local masters swimming programs: Many masters swimming programs offer morning workouts, and they might have access to pools that offer morning lap swimming hours.
* Contact pools directly: If you don't find a pool that meets your morning lap swimming needs, reach out to local pools directly and ask about their lap swimming hours and policies. They might be willing to accommodate your schedule or offer alternative solutions.

I hope this helps you find a pool that fits your busy schedule!
user: I've got a few more questions about the pool finder tools. Can I also filter by pool length and temperature? I used to swim competitively in college, and I'm looking for a 25-yard pool that's kept at around 78-80°F.
assistant: Yes, you can filter by pool length and temperature on many pool finder tools. Here's how:

1. **Swim.com**: On Swim.com, you can filter by "Pool Details" and select "Pool Length" to choose from various lengths, including 25 yards. You can also filter by "Water Temperature" and select a range (e.g., 78-80°F).
2. **PoolFinder**: PoolFinder allows you to filter by "Pool Characteristics" and select "Pool Length" to choose from various lengths, including 25 yards. You can also filter by "Pool Temperature" and select a range (e.g., 78-80°F).
3. **USA Swimming's Pool Finder**: On USA Swimming's Pool Finder, you can filter by "Pool Characteristics" and select "Pool Length" to choose from various lengths, including 25 yards. However, they don't have a specific filter for water temperature. You may need to contact the pool directly to inquire about their water temperature.

Additionally, you can also filter by other amenities that might be important to you, such as:

* **Lanes available**: Ensure the pool has enough lanes available for lap swimming during your preferred time.
* **Lane width**: If you're used to swimming in a specific lane width, you can filter by lane width to find pools that meet your preference.
* **Gutter system**: Some swimmers prefer pools with a specific gutter system, such as a bulkhead or a deck-level gutter.
* **Starting blocks**: If you're planning to do flip turns, you might want to filter by pools with starting blocks.
* **Shower and locker facilities**: Consider filtering by pools with modern shower and locker facilities, especially if you plan to swim regularly.

When searching, you can also try using keywords like "25-yard pool," "short course," "competition pool," or "lap pool" to find pools that are more likely to meet your needs.

Remember to always verify the pool's details with a phone call or visit to the pool's website to ensure they meet your requirements.
user: I'm also wondering if I can find pools that have swim teams or masters swimming programs that I can join. Do you know if the pool finder tools allow me to filter by that?
assistant: Many pool finder tools allow you to filter by pools with swim teams or masters swimming programs. Here's how:

1. **Swim.com**: On Swim.com, you can filter by "Programs & Teams" and select "Masters Swimming" or "Swim Team" to find pools with these programs. You can also filter by "USMS" (United States Masters Swimming) to find pools that are registered with USMS.
2. **PoolFinder**: PoolFinder allows you to filter by "Programs & Activities" and select "Masters Swimming" or "Swim Team" to find pools with these programs.
3. **USA Swimming's Pool Finder**: On USA Swimming's Pool Finder, you can filter by "Programs & Teams" and select "Masters Swimming" or "Swim Team" to find pools with these programs. You can also filter by "USMS" to find pools that are registered with USMS.

Additionally, you can also search for pools that are affiliated with specific swim organizations, such as:

* **USA Swimming**: The national governing body for swimming in the United States.
* **USMS**: United States Masters Swimming, a organization for adult swimmers.
* **YMCA**: Many YMCAs have swim teams and masters swimming programs.

When searching, you can also try using keywords like "masters swimming," "swim team," "USMS," or "USA Swimming" to find pools that are more likely to have these programs.

Some other tips:

* **Contact the pool directly**: Even if a pool doesn't show up in your search results, it's worth contacting them directly to ask about their swim teams or masters swimming programs. They might have programs that aren't listed online.
* **Check with local swim organizations**: Reach out to local swim organizations, such as your state or regional USMS chapter, to ask about pools with masters swimming programs in your area.
* **Look for online swim teams and clubs**: Join online forums, social media groups, or websites focused on swimming to connect with other swimmers and find pools with swim teams or masters swimming programs.

By filtering by pool amenities, programs, and affiliations, you should be able to find a pool that meets your needs and provides opportunities to join a swim team or masters swimming program.
user: I'd also like to ask about the pool's policy on kickboards, pull buoys, and other equipment. As someone who used to swim competitively in college, I'm used to having access to that kind of equipment during my workouts.
assistant: Many pool finder tools don't specifically ask about pool equipment, but you can still find out about a pool's policy on kickboards, pull buoys, and other equipment by:

1. **Contacting the pool directly**: Reach out to the pool via phone or email and ask about their policy on equipment usage. They can let you know what equipment is available, if it's included with membership or day passes, and if there are any specific rules or restrictions.
2. **Checking their website**: Sometimes, pools will list their equipment and amenities on their website. Look for a section on "Pool Amenities" or "Swim Equipment" to see if they mention kickboards, pull buoys, or other equipment.
3. **Asking about their masters swimming program**: If you're looking to join a masters swimming program, ask the coach or program administrator about the equipment they use during workouts. They can give you insight into what's available and what's included in the program.

Some questions to ask when inquiring about equipment:

* Do you have kickboards, pull buoys, and other swim equipment available for use during lap swimming hours?
* Is the equipment included with membership or day passes, or is there an additional fee?
* Are there any specific rules or restrictions on equipment usage, such as only using certain equipment during certain hours or with permission from the lifeguard?
* Do you have any equipment available for checkout or rental, or do I need to bring my own?
* Are there any plans to add new equipment or upgrade existing equipment in the near future?

By asking these questions, you can get a better sense of whether a pool's equipment policy will meet your needs and expectations.

Remember, it's always a good idea to confirm a pool's policies and amenities before visiting or joining, especially if you have specific requirements or preferences.
user: I'll also ask about their policy on lane sharing and circle swimming. As someone who's used to swimming competitively in college, I'm comfortable swimming in a fast-paced environment, but I want to make sure I'll have enough space to do my workouts effectively.
assistant: As a competitive swimmer, you're likely accustomed to swimming in a fast-paced environment, and lane sharing and circle swimming can be important considerations. Here's what you can ask about:

1. **Lane sharing**: Ask if the pool has a policy on lane sharing, especially during peak hours. Find out if they have designated lanes for fast, medium, and slow swimmers, and if they enforce these designations.
2. **Circle swimming**: Inquire about their circle swimming policy. Do they use a counterclockwise or clockwise rotation? Are there any specific rules or signals for passing or being passed by other swimmers?
3. **Lane availability**: Ask about the number of lanes available for lap swimming and if they're reserved for specific swimming levels (e.g., fast, medium, slow). Find out if they have any restrictions on lane usage during certain times of the day or week.
4. **Swimmer density**: Find out if the pool has a policy on swimmer density, meaning the number of swimmers allowed in each lane. This can impact your swimming experience, especially if you're used to swimming in a crowded college pool.
5. **Lane etiquette**: Ask about their expectations for lane etiquette, such as passing rules, swimming on the correct side of the lane, and respecting other swimmers' space.

Some sample questions to ask:

* What is your policy on lane sharing during peak hours?
* Do you have designated lanes for fast, medium, and slow swimmers?
* How do you handle circle swimming in the lanes? Is it counterclockwise or clockwise?
* Are there any specific rules or signals for passing or being passed by other swimmers?
* How many lanes are available for lap swimming, and are they reserved for specific swimming levels?
* Are there any restrictions on lane usage during certain times of the day or week?
* What is the typical swimmer density in each lane, and how do you enforce it?
* What are your expectations for lane etiquette, and how do you ensure swimmers follow these rules?

By asking these questions, you can get a sense of whether the pool's policies and environment will meet your needs and allow you to swim effectively.

responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor
responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.

structured.fullText: {"notes":[{"candidate_claim_hints":["User used to swim competitively in college","User wants to get back into swimming to stay active and relieve stress","User prefers morning lap swimming sessions","User wants a 25-yard pool kept around 78-80°F","User is interested in swim teams or masters swimming programs","User values access to kickboards, pull buoys, and other workout equipment","User wants lane sharing and circle swimming policies that provide enough space for effective workouts"],"confidence":0.95,"entity_refs":[{"entity_id":"entity:<0>","role":"primary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<1>","role":"subject"},{"entity_id":"entity:<2>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<3>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<10>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<13>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<14>","role":"secondary"}],"evidence_kind":"summarized","evidence_quote":"I'm looking for a 25-yard pool that's kept at around 78-80°F.","evidence_reason":"The quoted requirement is one explicit part of a broader repeated set of pool-search criteria in the target transcript.","importance":4,"keywords":["local pool","lap swimming","morning lap swimming","25-yard pool","78-80°F","masters swimming","kickboards","pull buoys","lane sharing","circle swimming"],"note_type":"context","rationale":"This combines multiple related preferences and their practical rationale for future pool-search assistance; each individual preference may also be claim-like, but the reusable value is the contextual selection profile and trade-off frame.","scope_json":{"basis":"summarized","kind":"entity","text":"Applies when helping the user evaluate or search for local lap swimming pools and programs."},"summary":"The user is looking for a local lap swimming pool that supports a return to structured swimming: morning lap hours, 25-yard length, 78–80°F water, possible swim team or masters program, access to training equipment, and lane policies that allow effective workouts in a fast-paced environment.","tags":["pool_search","lap_swimming","fitness","preferences"],"title":"Pool selection criteria for returning competitive swimmer","valid_from":"2023-05-23T09:22:00+03:00","valid_to":null},{"candidate_claim_hints":["Pool finder tools may allow filters for morning hours, pool length, programs, and teams","USA Swimming's Pool Finder may require direct contact for water temperature","Pool equipment policies often require direct contact or website checks"],"confidence":0.9,"entity_refs":[{"entity_id":"entity:<4>","role":"primary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<5>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<6>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<8>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<11>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<1>","role":"subject"}],"evidence_kind":"summarized","evidence_quote":"Remember to always verify the pool's details with a phone call or visit to the pool's website","evidence_reason":"The transcript repeatedly advises using pool finder filters, then contacting pools or checking websites for details not reliably exposed by tools.","importance":3,"keywords":["pool finder tools","Swim.com","PoolFinder","USA Swimming Pool Finder","verify details","contact pool directly","masters swimming","equipment policy","lane policy"],"note_type":"lesson","rationale":"This is a reusable procedure and search strategy rather than a single atomic fact; it captures how to handle incomplete pool finder data in future assistance.","scope_json":{"basis":"summarized","kind":"entity","text":"Applies to using pool finder tools and follow-up verification for lap swimming pool searches."},"summary":"When pool finder tools do not expose all desired filters, the practical workflow is to search/filter for known structured attributes first, then verify details directly through pool websites, calls, masters program contacts, or local swim organizations. This is especially relevant for equipment availability, water temperature, lane sharing, circle swimming, and program details that may not be fully listed online.","tags":["search_workflow","pool_search","verification"],"title":"Pool finder workflow for lap swimming search","valid_from":"2023-05-23T09:22:00+03:00","valid_to":null}]}
